texto: Snake's LullabyBrother, sister, flick your tongueand taste the flakes of autumn sun.Use these last few hours of goldto travel, travel toward the cold.Before your coils grow stiff and dull,your heartbeat slows to winter's lull,seek the sink of sheltered stonesthat safely cradle sleeping bones.Brother, sister, find the waysback to the deep and tranquil bays,and 'round each other twist and foldto weave a heavy cloak of cold.
explicacao: Citação de Joyce Sidman sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “Snake's LullabyBrother, sister, flick your tongueand taste the flakes of autumn sun.Use these las…” articula amor, hibernation, nature em registro de amor, com relevância para contextos como viagem e nature.

texto: What Do the Trees Know?What do the trees know? To bend when all the wild winds blow. Roots are deep and time is slow. All we grasp we must let go.What do the trees know? Buds can weather ice and snow. Dark gives way to sunlight's glow. Strength and stillness help us grow.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Joyce Sidman que provoca passagem do tempo e prioridades. Ao questionar “What Do the Trees Know?What do the trees know? To bend when all the wild winds blow. Roots are de…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre sucesso, nature, poetry, com foco em termino e reflexao-noturna.

texto: Dream of the Tundra SwanDusk felland the cold came creeping,cam prickling into our hearts.As we tucked beaksinto feathers and settled for sleep,our wings knew.That night, we dreamed the journey:ice-blue sky and the yodel of flight,the sun's pale wafer,the crisp drink of clouds.We dreamed ourselves so far aloftthat the earth curved beneath usand nothing sang but a whistling vee of light.When we woke, we were covered with snow.We rose in a billow of white.
